The two Isaacs together — both Syrian, both monastic, both associated with the deep interior life of the Eastern desert tradition — present the community with a double testimony about the life of prayer. Their common theme is the same: that the interior journey is not a flight from the world but an engagement with its deepest reality, that the monk who prays in his cell is doing something for the world that the world cannot do for itself, and that the silence of the cell is not emptiness but the fullness of a presence that overflows into the lives of all who are touched by the pray-er's intercession.

Isaac of Nineveh, Phocas the gardener-martyr, and Isaac the Syrian together on this twenty-second day present the community with the mystic, the martyr, and the monastic — three versions of the same fundamental orientation toward God. What Maskal proclaimed in fire and procession, these three embody in garden soil and desert silence: that the Cross is the axis of all things, that the love it reveals is inexhaustible, and that the life ordered around it is a life of inexhaustible fruitfulness.

23.title: 23 Meskerem — St. Solomon the King & The Assembly
23.narrative:
Meskerem

St. Solomon the King and Prophet (Monthly Commemoration)

The Conception of St. John the Baptist

St. Xanthippe the Apostolic

The twenty-third day of Meskerem belongs to St. Solomon the King in his monthly commemoration — and in the Ethiopian context, as has been noted through the previous months, Solomon carries a weight that no other Christian calendar can replicate. The Solomonic dynasty, the Kebra Nagast, the union with the Queen of Sheba, the birth of Menelik I and the bringing of the Ark to Ethiopia — these are not merely legendary narratives but the founding charter of Ethiopian Christian civilization, the story through which the Church understands its own divine election and its particular place in the economy of salvation. In Meskerem, the month of new beginnings, Solomon's monthly feast is a remembrance of origins — of the deep roots from which the tree of Ethiopian Christianity has grown across three thousand years of claimed continuity from the house of David.

The Conception of St. John the Baptist falls on the twenty-third of Meskerem — nine months before the Baptist's birthday on the twenty-fourth of Yekatit — and its placement in the calendar is an act of theological precision: the Church counts John's life from his conception, not from his birth, because the Gospel of Luke records that he leapt in the womb when Mary's greeting reached Elizabeth's ears. He was full of the Holy Spirit from the womb, as the angel had promised to his father Zechariah, and his prophetic vocation began before his birth. The Conception of John in Meskerem places the Forerunner's story at the very opening of the new year's narrative — the announcement that precedes all other announcements, the beginning of the beginning. In the post-Maskal month, with the Cross already blazing on the hilltops, the Conception of the one who would cry 'Behold the Lamb of God' is the year's most intimate preparation.

St. Xanthippe the Apostolic was a Spanish noblewoman who, according to tradition, was converted through the preaching of St. Paul and became one of the earliest and most committed members of the Western apostolic community. She is described as having shared in the apostolic mission with a zeal that placed her alongside the great women missionaries of the early Church — women whose stories the tradition has often preserved only partially, but whose contribution to the spread of the Gospel in the first generation was essential and irreplaceable. On the twenty-third day of Meskerem, Xanthippe's presence in the assembly is the Western Church's testimony that the Cross whose Ethiopian feast was celebrated on the seventeenth day belongs to Hispania as much as it belongs to the highlands of Shewa — that the trophy found by Helena is distributed to the entire Body.

Solomon, the Conception of John, and Xanthippe together on this twenty-third day weave the month's threads into a pattern of origins and preparations: the dynastic origin of the Church's deepest self-understanding, the conceived but unborn Forerunner who will one day cry out at the Jordan, and the noblewoman of the West who received the Cross's message from the apostle who carried it furthest. The new year is more than three weeks old, and Meskerem's golden light is maturing from the fresh brightness of Enkutatash into the richer, deeper glow of a month that has found its full identity.

24.title: 24 Meskerem — The Arrival in Egypt & The Assembly
24.narrative:
Meskerem

The Arrival of Christ in Egypt (Monthly Commemoration)

St. Thekla the Protomartyr of Women, Equal-to-the-Apostles

St. Silvanus of Gaza and Companions, Martyrs

The twenty-fourth day of Meskerem holds the monthly commemoration of the Arrival of Christ in Egypt — that sacred journey of the Holy Family which fills so much of the Coptic and Ethiopian sacred geography with the fragrance of the divine presence. This second appearance of the Egyptian theme in the same month as the first (celebrated on the twentieth) is not redundant but cumulative: the first commemorated the departure from Bethlehem, the flight from Herod; this one commemorates the arrival, the settling-in, the moment when the refugee family found shelter in the ancient land of bondage that had become, for them, a land of refuge. Egypt receives Christ as Egypt received the Patriarch Joseph — as a place that seems hostile from the outside but becomes, in the providence of God, the very place where the purposes of heaven are preserved and nurtured through the season of danger.

St. Thekla the Protomartyr of Women and Equal-to-the-Apostles is one of the most beloved and widely venerated women in the entire Eastern Christian tradition, and her feast on the twenty-fourth of Meskerem is among the great women's feasts of the year. She heard Paul preaching in Iconium, was transformed by the word she received, broke her engagement, and embarked on a missionary career of extraordinary breadth and courage — surviving multiple attempts to execute her, baptizing herself in extremity when water was available and no minister was present, and dying peacefully in extreme old age at Seleucia after decades of healing and teaching ministry. The early Church's regard for Thekla was such that she was venerated as virtually an apostle, and in some communities her memory was celebrated alongside Paul's as a co-worker in the apostolic mission rather than merely a disciple.

St. Silvanus of Gaza and his companions were Palestinian monastics who were seized from their communities and martyred during the early fourth century persecution. Silvanus was a bishop who had spent years as a monk before his consecration, and his death alongside his monastic brothers reflects the peculiar vulnerability of the Palestinian monastic communities of this era: communities too visible to be ignored, too faithful to be compromised, and too embedded in their particular landscape to be easily evacuated. His martyrdom at Gaza is a reminder that the Land which the Holy Family traversed on their flight to Egypt — the Land through which Philip ran to meet the Ethiopian eunuch — was itself a land of martyrs, a ground saturated with the blood of those who kept faith in the crossroads of empires.

The Arrival in Egypt, Thekla, and Silvanus together on the twenty-fourth day present the community with a theology of faithful arrival. The Holy Family arrived in Egypt as refugees and found it a place of divine preservation. Thekla arrived at the apostolic mission as an outsider to its official structures and found it a place of divine commissioning. Silvanus arrived at his final martyrdom through the long preparation of the monastic life and found it a place of completion. Every arrival the community makes — at the new year, at the post-Maskal month, at the daily prayer, at the year's final days — is an arrival in the same divine territory: the space where God receives those who come, and preserves what He receives.

25.title: 25 Meskerem — St. Tekle Haymanot & The Assembly
25.narrative:
Meskerem

St. Tekle Haymanot of Ethiopia (Monthly Commemoration)

St. Paphnutius the Confessor, Bishop

St. Euphrosyne of Alexandria, Monastic

The twenty-fifth day of Meskerem brings the monthly commemoration of St. Tekle Haymanot — the Light of Ethiopia, the apostle of the South, the founder of Debre Libanos — into the golden month of the new year's beginning. Having encountered his departure feast on the twenty-fifth of Nehase, the community now meets him again monthly in the first Meskerem, and the quality of this meeting is different: not the solemn commemoration of departure but the joyful claim of his ongoing intercession in the new year. Tekle Haymanot is present every month not as a memory but as a living intercessor, and his appearance in Meskerem's twenty-fifth day is an assurance that the great saint of the Ethiopian highlands is already praying for the year that has just begun — that his intercessions, like his one-legged vigil, are continuous and unyielding and fruitful beyond what calculation can compass.

St. Paphnutius the Confessor was one of the most revered bishops at the Council of Nicaea — a man who had been partially blinded and maimed in the Galerian persecution, who bore the marks of his suffering visibly on his body, and who was so respected for his witness that Constantine reportedly rose from his throne to embrace him when the Council assembled. Paphnutius is said to have argued successfully against imposing celibacy as a requirement for married clergy already in orders — a counsel of pastoral wisdom and human realism that the tradition has remembered alongside his theological contributions. At the Council of Nicaea he bore on his body the argument for orthodoxy that no opponent could dismiss: these were the wounds of a man who had endured more for the faith than any theoretical opponent could claim to know. On the twenty-fifth day of Meskerem, Paphnutius stands as the confessor who brought the knowledge of the body into the conciliar hall — who insisted that theology must be answerable to the lived experience of those who have borne its cost.

St. Euphrosyne of Alexandria is one of the tradition's most affecting feminine narratives of hidden holiness: a young Alexandrian woman who, on the eve of her arranged marriage, fled to a monastery and lived for thirty-eight years disguised as a male monk named Smaragdus — never once being detected — before revealing herself to her father, who had spent the same thirty-eight years mourning her disappearance, in her final days. The reunion between father and daughter at the moment of her death is one of the most tender scenes in the whole hagiographic tradition: thirty-eight years of mutual grief collapsed in a final recognition. Euphrosyne carries into Meskerem's assembly the testimony of those whose vocation required hiddenness, whose sanctity was most completely expressed in the concealment that allowed them to grow without the distortion of public attention.

Tekle Haymanot, Paphnutius, and Euphrosyne together on this twenty-fifth day of Meskerem present three modes of the complete Christian life: the visible apostolic missionary whose works fill the landscape of the nation; the wounded confessor whose body itself is a theological argument; and the hidden contemplative whose thirty-eight years of concealment produced a depth of holiness that her brief public revelation could not contain. The new year that began with the public joy of Enkutatash is being completed, in its twenty-fifth day, by the reminder that the same Spirit who fills the open fields with yellow daisies also fills the hidden cell with a light that the world cannot see — and that both forms of radiance are equally necessary to the full witness of the Church.

26.title: 26 Meskerem — St. Joshua son of Nun & The Assembly
26.narrative:
Meskerem

St. Joshua son of Nun (Monthly Commemoration)

St. John the Apostle and Evangelist (Departure and Translation)

The twenty-sixth day of Meskerem carries two immense figures whose joint commemoration on this single day creates one of the most theologically resonant juxtapositions in the entire calendar. St. Joshua son of Nun, the conqueror of Canaan and inheritor of Moses's mantle, is commemorated monthly — and as always, his presence in the calendar is the presence of the one who completes what his predecessor began, the one who brings the people through the Jordan and into the land of promise. But today Joshua shares his day with something extraordinary: the feast of the Departure and Translation of St. John the Apostle, the Beloved Disciple. This is not merely a coincidence of the calendar — it is the deep grammar of the liturgical tradition speaking: Joshua and John are the two great 'completers' of their respective covenants, and their joint feast on the twenty-sixth of Meskerem is the month's most profound theological statement.

The feast of John's Departure is unlike any other departure-feast in the calendar, for John's death remains, even in the tradition's most careful accounts, wrapped in mystery. He did not die a martyr's death by execution — alone among the apostles, he survived every attempt on his life. He lived to extreme old age in Ephesus, and the tradition records that he was taken to his burial by the community, and that the earth above his tomb was said to breathe, suggesting a dormition rather than a full death — a passing-over that anticipated the resurrection rather than submitting to it in the ordinary way. Some accounts hold that he was translated bodily, in the manner of Elijah, without tasting death. The tradition holds the question open, with a theological wisdom: John is the apostle of love, and love does not end; John is the apostle of the Word, and the Word is eternal; John is the disciple who was told, 'If I want him to remain until I come, what is that to you?' — and the Church has always understood that in some sense, John remains. His departure is framed as a translation into the age to come, the beginning of a presence more complete rather than an ending.

The pairing of Joshua and John on this twenty-sixth day invites a reading of Christian history as itself a crossing of the Jordan. Joshua led the people across the physical Jordan into the earthly Canaan; John, in his translation, crossed the ultimate Jordan into the heavenly Canaan — the Promised Land that is not made by hands, the country that Abraham saw from afar and greeted but did not reach in the body. What Joshua accomplished geographically and typologically, John accomplished personally and eschatologically. The end of the month's beginning — as the thematic directive calls this day — is precisely this: the year begun in Enkutatash's golden joy has received, in John's translation, its ultimate horizon. The year is oriented not merely toward the next Maskal or the next Filseta but toward the country that John has already entered, the country that Joshua's crossing was always a shadow of.

Joshua and John together on the twenty-sixth day of Meskerem seal the month's theology of arrival and transition. The month began with a crossing — from the old year to the new, from Pagumen to Meskerem — and it moves now toward its final days with a greater crossing in view: the crossing that John has already made, the crossing that the whole year's liturgical life is a preparation for. The golden light of Meskerem has always been eschatological light — not merely the light of spring flowers and new beginnings, but the light of the age to come, breaking into this age. John has gone ahead. Joshua has shown the way. The community that has walked through Enkutatash and Maskal and all twenty-six days of the new year's first month is being pointed, gently and firmly, toward the horizon that waits beyond all the feasts and all the fasts.

27.title: 27 Meskerem — The Monthly Crucifixion & The Assembly
27.narrative:
Meskerem

The Monthly Commemoration of the Holy Crucifixion

St. Callistratus and the Forty-Nine Martyrs

St. Epicharis the Martyr

On the twenty-seventh of Meskerem, the Church stands again before the Cross in the monthly Crucifixion commemoration — and in the month of Maskal, this monthly remembrance carries a unique quality. The Maskal Cross blazed on the hilltops ten days ago in royal triumph; today the same Cross is contemplated in its original and irreducible reality: the wood on which the Son of God died. The Demera fire is still warm in the memory; the Maskal processions are still vivid in the imagination; and now the Church strips away the celebration and stands before what is beneath all celebration — the bare wood, the nails, the cry of dereliction, the darkness at midday, the curtain torn, the blood and water. The monthly Crucifixion is the calendar's insistence that no feast, however glorious, can be permitted to replace the event it celebrates. Maskal is real, and its triumph is real; but it is the triumph of this, and the memory of this must not be softened by the triumph's brightness.

St. Callistratus and the Forty-Nine Martyrs are one of the great corporate martyrdom narratives of the early Church. Callistratus was a soldier who was discovered praying by his fellow soldiers at night, was thrown into the sea in a sack, was miraculously preserved and emerged unharmed, and was then — in the tradition's account — joined in his confession by forty-nine of his fellow soldiers who had witnessed the miracle and converted on the spot. They were subsequently executed together. The corporate quality of this martyrdom — one man's witness producing forty-nine further witnesses, one preserved life generating forty-nine surrendered lives — is one of the most powerful demonstrations in the entire martyrology of the contagious quality of holy courage. The Cross is not a solo act; it generates a community of the Cross, a fellowship of those who are willing to go where it leads.

St. Epicharis was a woman of Rome who was tortured for information about a conspiracy against the Emperor Nero — a conspiracy she may not even have been directly part of — and who endured the torture in silence, not betraying a single name. She was brought back on the second day of interrogation in a chair, her body broken, and continued to refuse. She finally strangled herself in the chair with a cloth from her garment to prevent further attempts to break her silence. Her story is a pagan woman's martyrdom in the ethical rather than the explicitly theological sense, yet the Church has recognized in her silence a form of the same refusal that the martyrs displayed before their persecutors. On the monthly Crucifixion day, Epicharis's unbreakable silence is the pagan world's testimony that the values the Cross embodies — the refusal to betray the innocent, the acceptance of suffering rather than complicity — are not exclusively Christian but are recognized, in moments of extremity, by every human soul that has kept its humanity intact.

The monthly Crucifixion, Callistratus and his forty-nine, and Epicharis together on the twenty-seventh of Meskerem present the Cross in its full complexity: the cosmic event of redemption, the community it generates through witness, and the human truth it recognizes even beyond its own boundaries. The golden light of Meskerem has been real and beautiful, and the Cross that shines within it is real and beautiful, and it is also the wood on which a man died in agony. The community that holds all of this together — triumph and cost, fire and blood, the golden daisy and the crown of thorns — is the community that has understood what the month of Meskerem is actually teaching.

28.title: 28 Meskerem — St. Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob & The Assembly
28.narrative:
Meskerem

The Holy Patriarchs Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob

St. Chariton the Confessor, Abbot

St. Isaac of Harpe, Desert Father

The twenty-eighth day of Meskerem brings the Holy Patriarchs — Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob — into the new year's assembly, and their arrival in the first month of the new cycle has a quality distinct from their appearance on the twenty-eighth of Nehase. In Nehase they came at the year's ending, as part of the great recapitulation. Here in Meskerem they come at the beginning, and they come as the founders of everything that follows: Abraham who went out in faith, Isaac who lay down on the mountain and rose again, Jacob who wrestled with the Angel of the Lord and received both a wound and a name. The new year begins with these three because the faith that the year will test is the faith that they established — a faith that moves into the unknown, that offers what is most precious, that does not let go of the divine presence until it has received the blessing.

St. Chariton the Confessor was a third-century pilgrim from Iconium who was captured by bandits on his way to Jerusalem, miraculously delivered when the bandit chief was killed by a snake (and the remaining bandits, terrified, fled), and who used the bandit cave as the nucleus for a monastic community — the first of three lavras he would found in the Judean desert. His story is one of the tradition's most beautiful examples of providential conversion: the planned pilgrimage becomes an unplanned captivity becomes an unplanned liberation becomes an unintended monastery. God's purposes for Chariton bore no resemblance to Chariton's purposes for himself, and yet God's purposes were better. On the twenty-eighth day of the new year, Chariton stands as the counsel against overly determined plans: hold the year's intentions loosely, because the cave in which you are detained may turn out to be the monastery in which you are formed.

St. Isaac of Harpe, the desert father whose name connects him to the great Isaac-tradition of the Syrian and Egyptian monasticism, brings the contemplative note into the twenty-eighth day's assembly alongside the Patriarchs and the reluctant-monk Chariton. Isaac of Harpe is associated with the tradition of the harp as an instrument of prayer — the Psalter sung in the cell, the sacred songs that David composed and the monks inherited, the music that is not entertainment but intercession. On the twenty-eighth day of Meskerem, in a month that has been filled with the extraordinary music of Maskal and Enkutatash, Isaac of Harpe reminds the community that the music of the great feasts descends from the music of the cell — that the liturgical splendor of the public celebration is sustained by the unceasing private prayer of those who sing in the hidden place.

Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, Chariton, and Isaac of Harpe together compose the twenty-eighth day's meditation on the faith that builds across generations and circumstances. The Patriarchs built it across centuries; Chariton built it across the unexpected conversion of a robbers' den; Isaac of Harpe built it note by note in the solitude of the cell. These are the three modes of the same faith: the dynastic, the providential, and the contemplative. The new year's community receives them as its teachers — the fathers who model the faith that makes new years worth beginning, the monk who trusts God's detours, and the singer who sustains the whole enterprise in the silence between the feasts.

29.title: 29 Meskerem — The Triple Feast & The Assembly
29.narrative:
Meskerem

The Triple Feast: Maskal, Qal and Timqat (Monthly Commemoration)

St. Cyriacus the Anchorite

St. Dada the Martyr

The twenty-ninth day of Meskerem holds the monthly Triple Feast — Maskal, Qal, and Timqat together — and in the month that has already celebrated Maskal in its fullness on the seventeenth day, this monthly recapitulation has a particular quality of integration. The Feast of the True Cross is revisited; the Covenant-Word (Qal) that underlies all the month's prophetic voices is gathered up; the Theophany-Baptism (Timqat) that is the sacramental entrance into the Cross's mystery is placed beside its fellows. In Meskerem the Triple Feast on the twenty-ninth arrives not as a reminder of something distant but as the deepening of something recently received: the community that walked through the Maskal bonfires twelve days ago now contemplates what it entered in that procession — not merely a cultural celebration but a sacramental participation in the finding of the Cross, the hearing of the divine Word, and the baptismal death-and-resurrection that the Theophany announces.

St. Cyriacus the Anchorite was one of the remarkable figures of the Palestinian desert tradition in the late fifth and early sixth centuries — a monk who lived to extreme old age, reportedly over a hundred years, in a series of increasingly remote locations, finally ending his days in a cave in the wilderness that his abbot, Euthymius the Great, had shown him decades earlier. He is the embodiment of the long, patient, faithful solitude that underlies and sustains the Church's more visible activities. During the ninety-plus years of his monastic life, Cyriacus outlasted empires, heresies, councils, and controversies — not by engaging them all directly but by remaining in his cell and praying, and by being available to those who sought him out with genuine spiritual need. On the twenty-ninth day of Meskerem, Cyriacus is the ancient witness that the year's new beginning requires the long patience of the anchorite as much as the bright enthusiasm of Enkutatash.

St. Dada the Martyr, from the Persian tradition of martyrdom under Shapur II, adds the Eastern Church's suffering witness to the day's assembly — the voice of a Christianity that celebrated no Maskal bonfires in the Sassanid empire, that had no Constantine to end its persecution, that survived through the sheer internal resilience of a community whose faith was its only resource. Dada's witness on the twenty-ninth of the month is the calendar's reminder that the golden light of Meskerem, which is a real light — genuinely celebratory, genuinely joyful — is also a light that shines most purely in communities where it has been most fiercely opposed. The Persian martyrs kept the Triple Feast's realities — the Cross, the Word, the Baptism — without any of the Triple Feast's cultural magnificence, and their keeping was as real as any highland bonfire.

The Triple Feast, Cyriacus, and Dada together on the twenty-ninth day bring Meskerem to its penultimate moment with a sweep that gathers everything the month has offered: the great sacramental mysteries in their monthly summary, the patience of the century-long anchorite, and the fidelity of the persecuted Eastern witness. One day remains. The new year's first month is almost complete, and the community that has traveled through it from Enkutatash to the Triple Feast's monthly recapitulation has been formed in the full shape of what the Ethiopian liturgical year asks of it: to begin in joy, to build toward the Cross's triumph, to receive the triple mystery, and to carry it all forward in the patience and the courage that the anchorite and the martyr alike embody.

30.title: 30 Meskerem — St. Gregory the Illuminator & The Assembly
30.narrative:
Meskerem

St. Gregory the Illuminator, Apostle of Armenia

Sts. Rhipsime and Gaiane and Companions, Martyrs

St. Mardonia the Martyr

The thirtieth and final day of Meskerem belongs to St. Gregory the Illuminator — the Apostle of Armenia, the man who converted a king, a kingdom, and a civilization, and who established one of the most ancient national Christian churches in the history of the world. Gregory's story begins in imprisonment: he was kept in a dungeon pit for thirteen years by King Tiridates III of Armenia, whom he had refused to renounce Christ before. After thirteen years, Tiridates went mad, was healed through Gregory's intercession, and converted — and the conversion of the king was the conversion of the nation. In 301 A.D. Armenia became the first country in the world to adopt Christianity as its state religion, three years before Constantine's conversion began the Christianization of Rome. On the last day of Meskerem — the month that began with Enkutatash and blazed with Maskal — Gregory the Illuminator stands as the supreme example of a new beginning that emerged from thirteen years of darkness. The illumination comes after the dungeon. The new year comes after Pagumen. The pattern is consistent, and the last day of the month seals it.

Sts. Rhipsime and Gaiane and their companions were the community of Christian women whose arrival in Armenia preceded Gregory's release from the pit and whose martyrdom at Tiridates's hands triggered the king's madness that Gregory then healed. They had fled the persecution in Rome and sought refuge in Armenia, where their piety and beauty attracted Tiridates's attention. When Rhipsime refused his advances, the king had the entire company massacred. The madness that followed was, in the tradition's reading, the immediate consequence of this act — the divine response to the slaughter of holy women who had sought nothing but sanctuary. Rhipsime and Gaiane are thus not merely Gregory's supporting cast; they are the seeds whose blood prepared the ground for Armenia's Christianization. The kingdom that Gregory illuminated was fertilized, first, by the blood of women who died refusing a king. Their feast on the last day of Meskerem speaks to the community about the invisible cost of every visible triumph.

St. Mardonia the Martyr adds a final witness to the day's assembly — one of the many Eastern martyrs whose individual story is less fully preserved but whose blood belongs to the same stream that produced Gregory's illumination and Rhipsime's witness. In the constellation of the last day's saints, Mardonia is the reminder that every great conversion and every celebrated Illuminator stands on the shoulders of the unnamed-but-real company of those who held the faith without recognition, whose suffering created the conditions for what history would record in more celebrated names.

On this thirtieth and final day of Meskerem, the first month of the new year closes with the Illuminator, the martyred women, and the anonymous martyr standing together in the month's closing light. The year began with Enkutatash's golden joy; the month blazed with Maskal's triumphal fire; and it ends with the testimony of a man who spent thirteen years in a dungeon before the illumination came. The community that has walked through all thirty days of Meskerem is being sent into the month of Tiqimt with this final counsel from Gregory's life: do not be deceived by the dungeon's darkness into thinking that the illumination will not come. It will come. It always comes. And when it comes, it comes as a new year — as Enkutatash, as the Crown of the Year, as the yellow daisies breaking across the highland hills in the early morning light of a world being made new.
